rules.description: Checks for usage of items through absolute paths, like std::env::current_dir.
rules.bad: let x = std::f64::consts::PI;

rules.description: Checks for comparisons where one side of the relation is either the minimum or maximum value for its type and warns if it involves a case that is always true or always false. Only integer and boolean types are checked.
Finds items imported through alloc when available through core.
rules.bad: use alloc::slice::from_ref;

rules.description: Checks for usage of the #[allow] attribute and suggests replacing it with the #[expect] attribute (See RFC 2383) This lint only warns outer attributes (#[allow]), as inner attributes (#![allow]) are usually used to enable or disable lints o
Checks for attributes that allow lints without a reason.
rules.bad: #![allow(clippy::some_lint)]

rules.description: Checks for ranges which almost include the entire range of letters from ‘a’ to ‘z’ or digits from ‘0’ to ‘9’, but don’t because they’re a half open range.
Checks for foo = bar; bar = foo sequences.
rules.bad:
a = b;
b = a;

rules.description: Checks for floating point literals that approximate constants which are defined in std::f32::consts or std::f64::consts, respectively, suggesting to use the predefined constant.
rules.bad:
let x = 3.14;
let y = 1_f64 / x;
rules.good:
let x = std::f32::consts::PI;
let y = std::f64::consts::FRAC_1_PI;

rules.description: Confirms that items are sorted in source files as per configuration.
rules.bad:
module-item-order-groupings = [
    [ "modules", [ "extern_crate", "mod", "foreign_mod" ], ],
    [ "use", [ "use", ], ],
    [ "everything_else", [ "macro", "global_asm", "static", "const", "ty_alias", "enum", "struct", "union", "trait", "trait_alias", "impl", "fn", ], ],
]

rules.description: . This lint warns when you use Arc with a type that does not implement Send or Sync.

Checks any kind of arithmetic operation of any type. Operators like +, -, * or << are usually capable of overflowing according to the Rust Reference, or can panic (/, %). Known safe built-in types like Wrapping or Saturating, floats, operat

Checks for usage of as conversions. Note that this lint is specialized in linting every single use of as regardless of whether good alternatives exist or not. If you want more precise lints for as, please consider using these separate lints
Checks for the usage of as *const _ or as *mut _ conversion using inferred type.
Checks for the result of a &self-taking as_ptr being cast to a mutable pointer.
Checks for the usage of as _ conversion using inferred type.
Checks for assert!(true) and assert!(false) calls.
rules.bad:
assert!(false)
assert!(true)
const B: bool = false;
assert!(B)

rules.description: Checks for assert!(r.is_ok()) or assert!(r.is_err()) calls.
rules.bad:
assert!(r.is_ok());
assert!(r.is_err());

rules.description: Checks for a = a op b or a = b commutative_op a patterns.
Checks for code like foo = bar.clone();
Checks for async blocks that yield values of types that can themselves be awaited.
Allows users to configure types which should not be held across await suspension points.
Checks for calls to await while holding a non-async-aware MutexGuard.
Checks for calls to await while holding a RefCell, Ref, or RefMut.

Checks for incompatible bit masks in comparisons. The formula for detecting if an expression of the type _ <bit_op> m <cmp_op> c (where <bit_op> is one of {&, |} and <cmp_op> is one of {!=, >=, >, !=, >=, >}) can be determined from the foll
Checks for the usage of the to_be_bytes method and/or the function from_be_bytes.
rules.bad:
let _x = 2i32.to_be_bytes();
let _y = 2i64.to_be_bytes();

rules.description: Checks for usage of _.and_then(|x| Some(y)), _.and_then(|x| Ok(y)) or _.or_else(|x| Err(y)).
Checks for warn/deny/forbid attributes targeting the whole clippy::restriction category.
rules.bad: #![deny(clippy::restriction)]

rules.description: Checks for if and match conditions that use blocks containing an expression, statements or conditions that use closures with blocks.
This lint warns about boolean comparisons in assert-like macros.
rules.bad:
assert_eq!("a".is_empty(), false);
assert_ne!("a".is_empty(), true);

rules.description: Checks for expressions of the form x == true, x != true and order comparisons such as x < true (or vice versa) and suggest using the variable directly.
rules.bad:
if x == true {}
if y == false {}

rules.description: Instead of using an if statement to convert a bool to an int, this lint suggests using a from() function or an as coercion.
Checks for the usage of &expr as *const T or &mut expr as *mut T, and suggest using &raw const or &raw mut instead.
Checks for &*(&T).
Checks for a borrow of a named constant with interior mutability.
Checks for usage of &Box<T> anywhere in the code. Check the Box documentation for more information.
rules.bad: fn foo(bar: &Box<T>) { ... }

rules.description: Checks for usage of Box<T> where T is a collection such as Vec anywhere in the code. Check the Box documentation for more information.
checks for Box::new(Default::default()), which can be written as Box::default().
rules.bad: let x: Box<String> = Box::new(Default::default());

rules.description: Checks for usage of Box<T> where an unboxed T would work fine.
rules.bad: fn foo(x: Box<u32>) {}

rules.description: Checks if the if and else block contain shared code that can be moved out of the blocks.
Warns if a generic shadows a built-in type.
Checks for hard to read slices of byte characters, that could be more easily expressed as a byte string.
It checks for str::bytes().count() and suggests replacing it with str::len().
rules.bad:
"hello".bytes().count();
String::from("hello").bytes().count();

rules.description: Checks for the use of .bytes().nth().
rules.bad: "Hello".bytes().nth(3);

rules.description: Checks to see if all common metadata is defined in Cargo.toml. See: https://rust-lang-nursery.github.io/api-guidelines/documentation.html#cargotoml-includes-all-common-metadata-c-metadata
rules.bad:
[package]
name = "clippy"
version = "0.0.212"
repository = "https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy"
readme = "README.md"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
keywords = ["clippy", "lint", "plugin"]
categories = ["development-tools", "development-tools::cargo-plugins"]

rules.description: Checks for calls to ends_with with possible file extensions and suggests to use a case-insensitive approach instead.
Checks for usage of the abs() method that cast the result to unsigned.
rules.bad:
let x: i32 = -42;
let y: u32 = x.abs() as u32;
rules.good:
let x: i32 = -42;
let y: u32 = x.unsigned_abs();

rules.description: Checks for casts from an enum tuple constructor to an integer.
rules.bad:
enum E { X(i32) };
let _ = E::X as usize;

rules.description: Checks for casts from an enum type to an integral type that will definitely truncate the value.
rules.bad:
enum E { X = 256 };
let _ = E::X as u8;

rules.description: Checks for casts between numeric types that can be replaced by safe conversion functions.
Checks for a known NaN float being cast to an integer

Checks for casts between numeric types that may truncate large values. This is expected behavior, so the cast is Allow by default. It suggests user either explicitly ignore the lint, or use try_from() and handle the truncation, default, or

Checks for casts from an unsigned type to a signed type of the same size, or possibly smaller due to target-dependent integers. Performing such a cast is a no-op for the compiler (that is, nothing is changed at the bit level), and the binar
rules.bad: let _ = u32::MAX as i32; // will yield a value of `-1`

rules.description: Checks for casts from any numeric type to a float type where the receiving type cannot store all values from the original type without rounding errors. This possible rounding is to be expected, so this lint is Allow by default. Basically, t
rules.bad:
let x = u64::MAX;
x as f64;

rules.description: Checks for casts, using as or pointer::cast, from a less strictly aligned pointer to a more strictly aligned pointer.

Checks for casts from a signed to an unsigned numeric type. In this case, negative values wrap around to large positive values, which can be quite surprising in practice. However, since the cast works as defined, this lint is Allow by defau
Checks for as casts between raw pointers to slices with differently sized elements.
Checks for a raw slice being cast to a slice pointer
rules.bad:
let _: *const [u8] = std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, len) as *const _;
let _: *mut [u8] = std::slice::from_raw_parts_mut(ptr, len) as *mut _;
rules.good:
let _: *const [u8] = std::ptr::slice_from_raw_parts(ptr, len);
let _: *mut [u8] = std::ptr::slice_from_raw_parts_mut(ptr, len);

rules.description: Checks for usage of cfg that excludes code from test builds. (i.e., #[cfg(not(test))])
rules.bad:
#[cfg(not(test))]
important_check(); // I'm not actually tested, but not including me will falsely increase coverage!

rules.description: Checks for usage of a character position yielded by .chars().enumerate() in a context where a byte index is expected, such as an argument to a specific str method or indexing into a str or String.
Checks for expressions where a character literal is cast to u8 and suggests using a byte literal instead.
rules.bad: 'x' as u8

rules.description: Checks for usage of _.chars().last() or _.chars().next_back() on a str to check if it ends with a given char.
rules.bad: name.chars().last() == Some('_') || name.chars().next_back() == Some('-');

rules.description: Checks for usage of .chars().next() on a str to check if it starts with a given char.
rules.bad:
let name = "foo";
if name.chars().next() == Some('_') {};

rules.description: Checks for explicit bounds checking when casting.
rules.bad: foo <= i32::MAX as u32;

rules.description: Checks for usage of .drain(..) for the sole purpose of clearing a container.
rules.bad:
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
v.drain(..);

rules.description: Checks for usage of .clone() on a Copy type.
rules.bad: 42u64.clone();

rules.description: Checks for usage of .clone() on a ref-counted pointer, (Rc, Arc, rc::Weak, or sync::Weak), and suggests calling Clone via unified function syntax instead (e.g., Rc::clone(foo)).
rules.bad:
let x = Rc::new(1);

x.clone();

rules.description: Checks for usage of cloned() on an Iterator or Option where copied() could be used instead.
rules.bad: [1, 2, 3].iter().cloned();

rules.description: Checks for slice references with cloned references such as &[f.clone()].
This lint checks for equality comparisons with ptr::null or ptr::null_mut
Checks for conversions to owned values just for the sake of a comparison.
rules.bad: if x.to_owned() == y {}

rules.description: Protects against unintended coercion of references to container types to &dyn Any when the container type dereferences to a dyn Any which could be directly referenced instead.
Checks for collapsible else { if ... } expressions that can be collapsed to else if ....
Checks for nested if statements which can be collapsed by &&-combining their conditions.

Finds nested match or if let expressions where the patterns may be “collapsed” together without adding any branches. Note that this lint is not intended to find all cases where nested match patterns can be merged, but only cases where mergi
Checks for consecutive calls to str::replace (2 or more) that can be collapsed into a single call.
rules.bad:
let hello = "hesuo worpd"
    .replace('s', "l")
    .replace("u", "l")
    .replace('p', "l");

rules.description: Checks for collections that are never queried.
Checks comparison chains written with if that can be rewritten with match and cmp.
Checks for comparing to an empty slice such as "" or [], and suggests using .is_empty() where applicable.
Checks for casts of a primitive method pointer like max/min to any integer type.
rules.bad: let _ = u16::max as usize;

rules.description: It identifies calls to .is_empty() on constant values.
Checks for types that implement Copy as well as Iterator.
Checks for usage of crate as opposed to $crate in a macro definition.
Checks usage of std::fs::create_dir and suggest using std::fs::create_dir_all instead.
rules.bad: std::fs::create_dir("foo");

rules.description: Checks for transmutes between a type T and *T.
rules.bad:
core::intrinsics::transmute(t) // where the result type is the same as
                               // `*t` or `&t`'s

rules.description: Checks for usage of the dbg! macro.
rules.bad: dbg!(true)

rules.description: Checks for function/method calls with a mutable parameter in debug_assert!, debug_assert_eq! and debug_assert_ne! macros.
Checks for decimal literals used as bit masks in bitwise operations.
rules.bad: let a = 14 & 6; // Bit pattern is not immediately clear

rules.description: Warns if there is a better representation for a numeric literal.
Checks for the declaration of named constant which contain interior mutability.
Checks for construction on unit struct using default.
It checks for std::iter::Empty::default() and suggests replacing it with std::iter::empty().
rules.bad:
let _ = std::iter::Empty::<usize>::default();
let iter: std::iter::Empty<usize> = std::iter::Empty::default();
rules.good:
let _ = std::iter::empty::<usize>();
let iter: std::iter::Empty<usize> = std::iter::empty();

rules.description: Checks for usage of unconstrained numeric literals which may cause default numeric fallback in type inference. Default numeric fallback means that if numeric types have not yet been bound to concrete types at the end of type inference, then
rules.bad:
let i = 10;
let f = 1.23;

rules.description: Checks for literal calls to Default::default().
rules.bad: let s: String = Default::default();

rules.description: Displays a warning when a union is declared with the default representation (without a #[repr(C)] attribute).
Checks for #[cfg_attr(rustfmt, rustfmt_skip)] and suggests to replace it with #[rustfmt::skip].
rules.bad:
#[cfg_attr(rustfmt, rustfmt_skip)]
fn main() { }

rules.description: Checks for #[cfg_attr(feature = "cargo-clippy", ...)] and for #[cfg(feature = "cargo-clippy")] and suggests to replace it with #[cfg_attr(clippy, ...)] or #[cfg(clippy)].
rules.bad:
#[cfg(feature = "cargo-clippy")]
struct Bar;

rules.description: Checks for #[deprecated] annotations with a since field that is not a valid semantic version. Also allows “TBD” to signal future deprecation.
rules.bad:
#[deprecated(since = "forever")]
fn something_else() { /* ... */ }

rules.description: Checks for usage of *& and *&mut in expressions.
rules.bad:
let a = f(*&mut b);
let c = *&d;
rules.good:
let a = f(b);
let c = d;

rules.description: Checks for slicing expressions which are equivalent to dereferencing the value.
rules.bad:
let vec = vec![1, 2, 3];
let slice = &vec[..];

rules.description: Detects manual std::default::Default implementations that are identical to a derived implementation.
Lints against manual PartialOrd and Ord implementations for types with a derived Ord or PartialOrd implementation.
rules.bad: k1.cmp(&k2) == k1.partial_cmp(&k2).unwrap()

rules.description: Checks for types that derive PartialEq and could implement Eq.
Lints against manual PartialEq implementations for types with a derived Hash implementation.
rules.bad: k1 == k2 ⇒ hash(k1) == hash(k2)

rules.description: Denies the configured fields in clippy.toml Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if fields are defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.bad:
disallowed-fields = [
    # Can use a string as the path of the disallowed field.
    "std::ops::Range::start",
    # Can also use an inline table with a `path` key.
    { path = "std::ops::Range::start" },
    # When using an inline table, can add a `reason` for why the field
    # is disallowed.
    { path = "std::ops::Range::start", reason = "The start of the range is not used" },
]

rules.description: Denies the configured macros in clippy.toml Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if macros are defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.bad:
disallowed-macros = [
    # Can use a string as the path of the disallowed macro.
    "std::print",
    # Can also use an inline table with a `path` key.
    { path = "std::println" },
    # When using an inline table, can add a `reason` for why the macro
    # is disallowed.
    { path = "serde::Serialize", reason = "no serializing" },
    # This would normally error if the path is incorrect, but with `allow-invalid` = `true`,
    # it will be silently ignored
    { path = "std::invalid_macro", reason = "use alternative instead", allow-invalid = true }
]

rules.description: Denies the configured methods and functions in clippy.toml Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if methods are defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.bad:
disallowed-methods = [
    # Can use a string as the path of the disallowed method.
    "std::boxed::Box::new",
    # Can also use an inline table with a `path` key.
    { path = "std::time::Instant::now" },
    # When using an inline table, can add a `reason` for why the method
    # is disallowed.
    { path = "std::vec::Vec::leak", reason = "no leaking memory" },
    # Can also add a `replacement` that will be offered as a suggestion.
    { path = "std::sync::Mutex::new", reason = "prefer faster & simpler non-poisonable mutex", replacement = "parking_lot::Mutex::new" },
    # This would normally error if the path is incorrect, but with `allow-invalid` = `true`,
    # it will be silently ignored
    { path = "std::fs::InvalidPath", reason = "use alternative instead", allow-invalid = true },
]
rules.good:
let mut xs = Vec::new(); // Vec::new is _not_ disallowed in the config.
xs.push(123); // Vec::push is _not_ disallowed in the config.

rules.description: Checks for usage of disallowed names for variables, such as foo.
rules.bad: let foo = 3.14;

rules.description: Checks for usage of unicode scripts other than those explicitly allowed by the lint config. This lint doesn’t take into account non-text scripts such as Unknown and Linear_A. It also ignores the Common script type. While configuring, be sur
Denies the configured types in clippy.toml. Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if types are defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.bad:
disallowed-types = [
    # Can use a string as the path of the disallowed type.
    "std::collections::BTreeMap",
    # Can also use an inline table with a `path` key.
    { path = "std::net::TcpListener" },
    # When using an inline table, can add a `reason` for why the type
    # is disallowed.
    { path = "std::net::Ipv4Addr", reason = "no IPv4 allowed" },
    # Can also add a `replacement` that will be offered as a suggestion.
    { path = "std::sync::Mutex", reason = "prefer faster & simpler non-poisonable mutex", replacement = "parking_lot::Mutex" },
    # This would normally error if the path is incorrect, but with `allow-invalid` = `true`,
    # it will be silently ignored
    { path = "std::invalid::Type", reason = "use alternative instead", allow-invalid = true }
]
rules.good:
// A similar type that is allowed by the config
use std::collections::HashMap;

rules.description: Checks for diverging calls that are not match arms or statements.
Checks the doc comments have unbroken links, mostly caused by bad formatted links such as broken across multiple lines.
Detects doc comment linebreaks that use double spaces to separate lines, instead of back-slash (\).
Checks if included files in doc comments are included only for cfg(doc).
rules.bad: #![doc = include_str!("some_file.md")]

rules.description: In CommonMark Markdown, the language used to write doc comments, a paragraph nested within a list or block quote does not need any line after the first one to be indented or marked. The specification calls this a “lazy paragraph continuatio
Checks for links with code directly adjacent to code text: [`MyItem`]`<`[`u32`]`>`.
rules.bad: //! [`first`](x)`second`

rules.description: Detects the syntax ['foo'] in documentation comments (notice quotes instead of backticks) outside of code blocks
rules.bad:
/// See also: ['foo']
fn bar() {}

rules.description: Checks for the presence of _, :: or camel-case words outside ticks in documentation.
Warns if a link reference definition appears at the start of a list item or quote.
rules.bad: //! - [link]: description

rules.description: Detects overindented list items in doc comments where the continuation lines are indented more than necessary.
rules.bad:
/// - This is the first item in a list
///      and this line is overindented.

rules.description: Checks for doc comments whose paragraphs do not end with a period or another punctuation mark. Various Markdowns constructs are taken into account to avoid false positives.

Detects syntax that looks like a footnote reference. Rustdoc footnotes are compatible with GitHub-Flavored Markdown (GFM). GFM does not parse a footnote reference unless its definition also exists. This lint checks for footnote references w
rules.bad:
/// This is not a footnote[^1], because no definition exists.
fn my_fn() {}

rules.description: Checks for double comparisons that could be simplified to a single expression.
rules.bad: if x == y || x < y {}

rules.description: Checks for Iterator::last being called on a DoubleEndedIterator, which can be replaced with DoubleEndedIterator::next_back.
rules.bad: let last_arg = "echo hello world".split(' ').last();

rules.description: Checks for a #[must_use] attribute without further information on functions and methods that return a type already marked as #[must_use].
Checks for unnecessary double parentheses.

Checks for calls to .drain() that clear the collection, immediately followed by a call to .collect(). “Collection” in this context refers to any type with a drain method: Vec, VecDeque, BinaryHeap, HashSet,HashMap, String
Checks for calls to std::mem::drop with a value that does not implement Drop.
Checks for files that are included as modules multiple times.
Checks for function arguments having the similar names differing by an underscore.
rules.bad: fn foo(a: i32, _a: i32) {}

rules.description: Checks for attributes that appear two or more times.
Checks for instances where a std::time::Duration is constructed using a smaller time unit when the value could be expressed more clearly using a larger unit.
Checks for calculation of subsecond microseconds or milliseconds from other Duration methods.
rules.bad:
let micros = duration.subsec_nanos() / 1_000;
let millis = duration.subsec_nanos() / 1_000_000;
rules.good:
let micros = duration.subsec_micros();
let millis = duration.subsec_millis();

rules.description: Checks for integer validity checks, followed by a transmute that is (incorrectly) evaluated eagerly (e.g. using bool::then_some).
Checks for lifetime annotations which can be replaced with anonymous lifetimes ('_).
Checks for usage of if expressions with an else if branch, but without a final else branch.
Detects documentation that is empty.
Checks for empty Drop implementations.
Finds enum variants without fields that are declared with empty brackets.

Checks for enums with no variants, which therefore are uninhabited types (cannot be instantiated). As of this writing, the never_type is still a nightly-only experimental API. Therefore, this lint is only triggered if #![feature(never_type)
rules.bad: enum CannotExist {}

rules.description: Checks for empty lines after doc comments.
Checks for empty lines after outer attributes
rules.bad:
#[allow(dead_code)]

fn not_quite_good_code() {}

rules.description: Checks for empty loop expressions.
rules.bad: loop {}

rules.description: Finds structs without fields (a so-called “empty struct”) that are declared with brackets.
rules.bad:
struct Cookie {}
struct Biscuit();

rules.description: Checks for C-like enumerations that are repr(isize/usize) and have values that don’t fit into an i32.
Checks for use Enum::*.
rules.bad:
use std::cmp::Ordering::*;

foo(Less);

rules.description: Detects enumeration variants that are prefixed or suffixed by the same characters.
Checks for equal operands to comparison, logical and bitwise, difference and division binary operators (==, >, etc., &&, ||, &, |, ^, - and /).
Checks for pattern matchings that can be expressed using equality.
Checks for erasing operations, e.g., x * 0.
Checks for .err().expect() calls on the Result type.
rules.bad:
let x: Result<u32, &str> = Ok(10);
x.err().expect("Testing err().expect()");
rules.good:
let x: Result<u32, &str> = Ok(10);
x.expect_err("Testing expect_err");

rules.description: Checks for types named Error that implement Error.
Checks for blocks which are nested beyond a certain threshold. Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if a maximum nesting level is defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.bad: excessive-nesting-threshold = 3

rules.description: Checks for float literals with a precision greater than that supported by the underlying type. The lint is suppressed for literals with over const_literal_digits_threshold digits.
Warns on any exported enums that are not tagged #[non_exhaustive]
Warns on any exported structs that are not tagged #[non_exhaustive]
Detects calls to the exit() function that are not in the main function. Calls to exit() immediately terminate the program.
Checks for calls to .expect(&format!(...)), .expect(foo(..)), etc., and suggests to use unwrap_or_else instead
Checks for .expect() or .expect_err() calls on Results and .expect() call on Options.
rules.bad:
option.expect("one");
result.expect("one");

rules.description: Checks for explicit Clone implementations for Copy types.
Checks for dereferencing expressions which would be covered by auto-deref.
rules.bad:
let x = String::new();
let y: &str = &*x;

rules.description: Checks for loops over slices with an explicit counter and suggests the use of .enumerate().
Checks for explicit deref() or deref_mut() method calls. Doesn’t lint inside the implementation of the Deref or DerefMut traits.
rules.good:
let a: &mut String = &mut String::from("foo");
let b = &*a;

rules.description: Checks for loops on y.into_iter() where y will do, and suggests the latter.
Checks for loops on x.iter() where &x will do, and suggests the latter.
Checks for usage of write!() / writeln()! which can be replaced with (e)print!() / (e)println!()
rules.bad:
writeln!(&mut std::io::stderr(), "foo: {:?}", bar).unwrap();
writeln!(&mut std::io::stdout(), "foo: {:?}", bar).unwrap();

rules.description: Checks for occurrences where one vector gets extended instead of append
Checks for lifetimes in generics that are never used anywhere else.
Checks for type parameters in generics that are never used anywhere else.
Checks for impls of From<..> that contain panic!() or unwrap()
Checks for immediate reassignment of fields initialized with Default::default().
rules.bad:
let mut a: A = Default::default();
a.i = 42;

rules.description: Checks for usage of scoped visibility modifiers, like pub(crate), on fields. These make a field visible within a scope between public and private.
Checks for FileType::is_file().
Checks for usage of bool::then in Iterator::filter_map.
rules.bad: _ = v.into_iter().filter_map(|i| (i % 2 == 0).then(|| really_expensive_fn(i)));

rules.description: Checks for usage of filter_map(|x| x).
rules.bad: iter.filter_map(|x| x);

rules.description: Checks for usage of _.filter_map(_).next().
rules.bad: (0..3).filter_map(|x| if x == 2 { Some(x) } else { None }).next();

rules.description: Checks for usage of _.filter(_).next().
rules.bad: vec.iter().filter(|x| **x == 0).next();

rules.description: Checks for usage of flat_map(|x| x).
rules.bad: iter.flat_map(|x| x);

rules.description: Checks for usage of Iterator::flat_map() where filter_map() could be used instead.
rules.bad: let nums: Vec<i32> = ["1", "2", "whee!"].iter().flat_map(|x| x.parse().ok()).collect();

rules.description: Checks for float arithmetic.
Checks for (in-)equality comparisons on floating-point values (apart from zero), except in functions called *eq* (which probably implement equality for a type involving floats).
Checks for (in-)equality comparisons on constant floating-point values (apart from zero), except in functions called *eq* (which probably implement equality for a type involving floats).
Checks for statements of the form (a - b) < f32::EPSILON or (a - b) < f64::EPSILON. Note the missing .abs().
Checks for excessive use of bools in function definitions.
rules.bad: fn f(is_round: bool, is_hot: bool) { ... }

rules.description: Checks for casts of function pointers to something other than usize.
rules.bad:
fn fun() -> i32 { 1 }
let _ = fun as i64;

rules.description: Checks for casts of a function pointer to any integer type.
Checks for casts of a function pointer to a numeric type not wide enough to store an address.
Checks for iterating a map (HashMap or BTreeMap) and ignoring either the keys or values.
Checks for calls to std::mem::forget with a value that does not implement Drop.
Checks for usage of .map(|_| format!(..)).collect::<String>().
Detects format! within the arguments of another macro that does formatting such as format! itself, write! or println!. Suggests inlining the format! call.
rules.bad: println!("error: {}", format!("something failed at {}", Location::caller()));

rules.description: Detects cases where the result of a format! call is appended to an existing String.
Checks for outer doc comments written with 4 forward slashes (////).
Checks for from_iter() function calls on types that implement the FromIterator trait.
Searches for implementations of the Into<..> trait and suggests to implement From<..> instead.
Checks if we’re passing a c_void raw pointer to {Box,Rc,Arc,Weak}::from_raw(_)
rules.bad:
let ptr = Box::into_raw(Box::new(42usize)) as *mut c_void;
let _ = unsafe { Box::from_raw(ptr) };

rules.description: Checks for function invocations of the form primitive::from_str_radix(s, 10)
rules.bad:
let input: &str = get_input();
let num = u16::from_str_radix(input, 10)?;
rules.good:
let input: &str = get_input();
let num: u16 = input.parse()?;

rules.description: This lint requires Future implementations returned from functions and methods to implement the Send marker trait, ignoring type parameters. If a function is generic and its Future conditionally implements Send based on a generic parameter t
rules.bad: async fn not_send(bytes: std::rc::Rc<[u8]>) {}

rules.description: Checks for usage of x.get(0) instead of x.first() or x.front().
rules.bad:
let x = vec![2, 3, 5];
let first_element = x.get(0);
rules.good:
let x = vec![2, 3, 5];
let first_element = x.first();

rules.description: Checks for usage of x.get(x.len() - 1) instead of x.last().
rules.bad:
let x = vec![2, 3, 5];
let last_element = x.get(x.len() - 1);
rules.good:
let x = vec![2, 3, 5];
let last_element = x.last();

rules.description: Checks for usage of .get().unwrap() (or .get_mut().unwrap) on a standard library type which implements Index
Checks for the usage of the to_ne_bytes method and/or the function from_ne_bytes.
rules.bad:
let _x = 2i32.to_ne_bytes();
let _y = 2i64.to_ne_bytes();

rules.description: Checks for identity operations, e.g., x + 0.
Checks for Mutex::lock calls in if let expression with lock calls in any of the else blocks.
Checks for usage of ! or != in an if condition with an else branch.
Checks for if/else with the same body as the then part and the else part.
Checks for if-else that could be written using either bool::then or bool::then_some.
Checks for consecutive ifs with the same condition.
Checks for ignored tests without messages.
Checks for usage of _ in patterns of type ().

This lint is concerned with the semantics of Borrow and Hash for a type that implements all three of Hash, Borrow<str> and Borrow<[u8]> as it is impossible to satisfy the semantics of Borrow and Hash for both Borrow<str> and Borrow<[u8]>.
Lints when impl Trait is being used in a function’s parameters.
Checks for the usage of _.to_owned(), vec.to_vec(), or similar when calling _.clone() would be clearer.
Checks for public impl or fn missing generalization over different hashers and implicitly defaulting to the default hashing algorithm (SipHash).
rules.bad:
impl<K: Hash + Eq, V> Serialize for HashMap<K, V> { }

pub fn foo(map: &mut HashMap<i32, i32>) { }

rules.description: Checks for missing return statements at the end of a block.
Checks for implicit saturating addition.
Checks for implicit saturating subtraction.
rules.good:
let mut i: u32 = end - start;

i = i.saturating_sub(1);

rules.description: Looks for bounds in impl Trait in return position that are implied by other bounds. This can happen when a trait is specified that another trait already has as a supertrait (e.g. fn() -> impl Deref + DerefMut<Target = i32> has an unnecessar
Checks for double comparisons that can never succeed
rules.bad: if status_code <= 400 && status_code > 500 {}

rules.description: Looks for floating-point expressions that can be expressed using built-in methods to improve accuracy at the cost of performance.
This lint checks that no function newer than the defined MSRV (minimum supported rust version) is used in the crate.
Warns if an integral or floating-point constant is grouped inconsistently with underscores.
Checks for struct constructors where the order of the field init in the constructor is inconsistent with the order in the struct definition.
The lint checks for slice bindings in patterns that are only used to access individual slice values.
Checks for usage of indexing or slicing that may panic at runtime. This lint does not report on indexing or slicing operations that always panic, out_of_bounds_indexing already handles those cases.

Checks for bit masks in comparisons which can be removed without changing the outcome. The basic structure can be seen in the following table: ComparisonBit OpExampleequals > / <=| / ^x | 2 > 3x > 3 < / >=| / ^x ^ 1 < 4x < 4
Checks if both .write(true) and .append(true) methods are called on a same OpenOptions.
Checks for usage of .to_string() on an &&T where T implements ToString directly (like &&str or &&String).
Checks for matches being used to destructure a single-variant enum or tuple struct where a let will suffice.
Finds manual impls of TryFrom with infallible error types.
Checks for iteration that is guaranteed to be infinite.
rules.bad:
use std::iter;

iter::repeat(1_u8).collect::<Vec<_>>();

rules.description: Checks for infinite loops in a function where the return type is not ! and lint accordingly.
Checks for the definition of inherent methods with a signature of to_string(&self) -> String.
Checks for the definition of inherent methods with a signature of to_string(&self) -> String and if the type implementing this method also implements the Display trait.
Checks for tuple structs initialized with field syntax. It will however not lint if a base initializer is present. The lint will also ignore code in macros.
Checks for items annotated with #[inline(always)], unless the annotated function is empty or simply panics.
rules.bad:
#[inline(always)]
fn not_quite_hot_code(..) { ... }

rules.description: Checks for usage of AT&T x86 assembly syntax.
rules.bad: asm!("lea ({}), {}", in(reg) ptr, lateout(reg) _, options(att_syntax));

rules.description: Checks for usage of Intel x86 assembly syntax.
rules.bad: asm!("lea {}, [{}]", lateout(reg) _, in(reg) ptr);

rules.description: Checks for #[inline] on trait methods without bodies
Checks for usage of inspect().for_each().
Checks for usage of x >= y + 1 or x - 1 >= y (and <=) in a block
rules.bad: if x >= y + 1 {}

rules.description: Checks for division of integers
rules.bad:
let x = 3 / 2;
println!("{}", x);

rules.description: Checks for the usage of division (/) and remainder (%) operations when performed on any integer types using the default Div and Rem trait implementations.
rules.bad: let my_div = 10 / 2;

rules.description: Checks for into_iter calls on references which should be replaced by iter or iter_mut.
rules.bad: (&vec).into_iter();

rules.description: This is the opposite of the iter_without_into_iter lint. It looks for IntoIterator for (&|&mut) Type implementations without an inherent iter or iter_mut method on the type or on any of the types in its Deref chain.
Checks regex creation (with Regex::new, RegexBuilder::new, or RegexSet::new) for correct regex syntax.
rules.bad: Regex::new("(")

rules.description: Checks for comparisons where the relation is always either true or false, but where one side has been upcast so that the comparison is necessary. Only integer types are checked.
Checks for comparisons between integers, followed by subtracting the greater value from the lower one.

rules.bad:
use std::io;
let _ = io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::Other, "bad".to_string());

rules.description: Checks for IP addresses that could be replaced with predefined constants such as Ipv4Addr::new(127, 0, 0, 1) instead of using the appropriate constants.
Finds usages of char::is_digit that can be replaced with is_ascii_digit or is_ascii_hexdigit.
Checks for items declared after some statement in a block.
Triggers if an item is declared after the testing module marked with #[cfg(test)].
Checks for the use of .cloned().collect() on slice to create a Vec.
rules.bad:
let s = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
let s2: Vec<isize> = s[..].iter().cloned().collect();

rules.description: Checks for the use of .iter().count().
Checks for usage of .filter(Result::is_ok) that may be replaced with a .flatten() call. This lint will require additional changes to the follow-up calls as it affects the type.
rules.bad: vec![Ok::<i32, String>(1)].into_iter().filter(Result::is_ok);

rules.description: Checks for usage of .filter(Option::is_some) that may be replaced with a .flatten() call. This lint will require additional changes to the follow-up calls as it affects the type.
rules.bad: vec![Some(1)].into_iter().filter(Option::is_some);

rules.description: Checks for iterating a map (HashMap or BTreeMap) and ignoring either the keys or values.
rules.bad:
let map: HashMap<u32, u32> = HashMap::new();
let values = map.iter().map(|(_, value)| value).collect::<Vec<_>>();
rules.good:
let map: HashMap<u32, u32> = HashMap::new();
let values = map.values().collect::<Vec<_>>();

rules.description: Checks for loops on x.next().
Checks for usage of iter().next() on a Slice or an Array
rules.bad:
a[2..].iter().next();
b.iter().next();

rules.description: Detects methods named iter or iter_mut that do not have a return type that implements Iterator.
Checks for usage of .iter().nth()/.iter_mut().nth() on standard library types that have equivalent .get()/.get_mut() methods.
Checks for the use of iter.nth(0).
rules.bad: let x = s.iter().nth(0);

rules.description: Checks for calls to iter, iter_mut or into_iter on empty collections
Checks for calls to iter, iter_mut or into_iter on collections containing a single item
rules.bad:
let a = [123].iter();
let b = Some(123).into_iter();

rules.description: Looks for iterator combinator calls such as .take(x) or .skip(x) where x is greater than the amount of items that an iterator will produce.
rules.bad: for _ in [1, 2, 3].iter().take(4) {}

rules.description: This is a restriction lint which prevents the use of hash types (i.e., HashSet and HashMap) in for loops.
rules.bad:
let my_map = std::collections::HashMap::<i32, String>::new();
    for (key, value) in my_map { /* ... */ }

rules.description: Checks for usage of _.cloned().<func>() where call to .cloned() can be postponed.
rules.bad:
vec.iter().cloned().take(10);
vec.iter().cloned().last();

rules.description: Checks for usage of .skip(x).next() on iterators.
Checks for usage of .skip(0) on iterators.
Checks for usage of .drain(..) on Vec and VecDeque for iteration.
rules.bad:
let mut foo = vec![0, 1, 2, 3];
let bar: HashSet<usize> = foo.drain(..).collect();
rules.good:
let foo = vec![0, 1, 2, 3];
let bar: HashSet<usize> = foo.into_iter().collect();

rules.description: Looks for iter and iter_mut methods without an associated IntoIterator for (&|&mut) Type implementation.
Checks for calling .step_by(0) on iterators which panics.
Checks for calls to Path::join that start with a path separator (\\ or /).
Checks if you have variables whose name consists of just underscores and digits.
Checks for large const arrays that should be defined as static instead.
Warns if the digits of an integral or floating-point constant are grouped into groups that are too large.
Checks for large size differences between variants on enums.
It checks for the size of a Future created by async fn or async {}.
Checks for the inclusion of large files via include_bytes!() or include_str!().
rules.bad:
let included_str = include_str!("very_large_file.txt");
let included_bytes = include_bytes!("very_large_file.txt");

rules.description: Checks for local arrays that may be too large.

Checks for functions that use a lot of stack space. This often happens when constructing a large type, such as an array with a lot of elements, or constructing many smaller-but-still-large structs, or copying around a lot of large types. Th

Checks for functions taking arguments by value, where the argument type is Copy and large enough to be worth considering passing by reference. Does not trigger if the function is being exported, because that might induce API breakage, if th
Checks for usage of <integer>::max_value(), std::<integer>::MAX, std::<float>::EPSILON, etc.
rules.bad: let eps = std::f32::EPSILON;

rules.description: Checks for items that implement .len() but not .is_empty().
Checks for getting the length of something via .len() just to compare to zero, and suggests using .is_empty() where applicable.
Checks for let-bindings, which are subsequently returned.
Checks for let _ = <expr> where the resulting type of expr implements Future
Checks for let _ = sync_lock. This supports mutex and rwlock in parking_lot. For std locks see the rustc lint let_underscore_lock
rules.bad: let _ = mutex.lock();

rules.description: Checks for let _ = <expr> where expr is #[must_use]
Checks for let _ = <expr> without a type annotation, and suggests to either provide one, or remove the let keyword altogether.
Checks for binding a unit value.
Detects when a variable is declared with an explicit type of _.
rules.bad: let my_number: _ = 1;

rules.description: Checks for usage of lines.filter_map(Result::ok) or lines.flat_map(Result::ok) when lines has type std::io::Lines.
rules.good:
let mut lines = BufReader::new(File::open("some-path")?).lines().map_while(Result::ok);
let first_line: Option<String> = lines.next();

rules.description: Checks for usage of any LinkedList, suggesting to use a Vec or a VecDeque (formerly called RingBuf).
rules.bad: let x: LinkedList<usize> = LinkedList::new();

rules.description: Checks for lint groups with the same priority as lints in the Cargo.toml [lints] table. This lint will be removed once cargo#12918 is resolved.
rules.bad:
[lints.clippy]
pedantic = "warn"
similar_names = "allow"

rules.description: Checks if string literals have formatting arguments outside of macros using them (like format!).
Checks for the usage of the to_le_bytes method and/or the function from_le_bytes.
rules.bad:
let _x = 2i32.to_le_bytes();
let _y = 2i64.to_le_bytes();

rules.description: Checks for whole number float literals that cannot be represented as the underlying type without loss.
Looks for macros that expand metavariables in an unsafe block.
Checks for #[macro_use] use....
Checks for recursion using the entrypoint.
Detects patterns like if a > b { a - b } else { b - a } and suggests using a.abs_diff(b).
Detects if-then-panic! that can be replaced with assert!.
rules.good:
let sad_people: Vec<&str> = vec![];
assert!(sad_people.is_empty(), "there are sad people: {:?}", sad_people);

rules.description: It checks for manual implementations of async functions.
rules.bad:
use std::future::Future;

fn foo() -> impl Future<Output = i32> { async { 42 } }

rules.description: Checks for usage of size_of::<T>() * 8 when T::BITS is available.
rules.bad: size_of::<usize>() * 8;

rules.description: Checks for the manual creation of C strings (a string with a NUL byte at the end), either through one of the CStr constructor functions, or more plainly by calling .as_ptr() on a (byte) string literal with a hardcoded \0 byte at the end.
Detects manual zero checks before dividing integers, such as if x != 0 { y / x }.
Identifies good opportunities for a clamp function from std or core, and suggests using it.
Checks for usage of iter().any() on slices when it can be replaced with contains() and suggests doing so.
Checks for casts of small constant literals or mem::align_of results to raw pointers.
Checks for an expression like (x + (y - 1)) / y which is a common manual reimplementation of x.div_ceil(y).
Checks for usage of match which could be implemented using filter
Checks for usage of _.filter(_).map(_) that can be written more simply as filter_map(_).
Checks for manual implementations of Iterator::find
Checks for usage of _.find(_).map(_) that can be written more simply as find_map(_).
Checks for unnecessary if let usage in a for loop where only the Some or Ok variant of the iterator element is used.
Checks for cases where BuildHasher::hash_one can be used.
Checks for manual case-insensitive ASCII comparison.
Checks for expressions like N - x.leading_zeros() (where N is one less than bit width of x) or x.ilog(2), which are manual reimplementations of x.ilog2()
Checks for uses of map which return the original item.
rules.bad: let x = Some(0).map(|x| { println!("{x}"); x });

rules.description: Lints subtraction between Instant::now() and another Instant.
Suggests to use dedicated built-in methods, is_ascii_(lowercase|uppercase|digit|hexdigit) for checking on corresponding ascii range
Checks for manual is_finite reimplementations (i.e., x != <float>::INFINITY && x != <float>::NEG_INFINITY).
rules.bad:
if x != f32::INFINITY && x != f32::NEG_INFINITY {}
if x.abs() < f32::INFINITY {}

rules.description: Checks for manual is_infinite reimplementations (i.e., x == <float>::INFINITY || x == <float>::NEG_INFINITY).
rules.bad: if x == f32::INFINITY || x == f32::NEG_INFINITY {}

rules.description: Checks for manual implementation of .is_multiple_of() on unsigned integer types.
Checks for expressions like x.count_ones() == 1 or x & (x - 1) == 0, with x and unsigned integer, which may be manual reimplementations of x.is_power_of_two().
rules.bad:
let a: u32 = 4;
let result = a.count_ones() == 1;
rules.good:
let a: u32 = 4;
let result = a.is_power_of_two();

rules.description: Checks for usage of option.map(f).unwrap_or_default() and result.map(f).unwrap_or_default() where f is a function or closure that returns the bool type. Also checks for equality comparisons like option.map(f) == Some(true) and result.map(f)
Warn of cases where let...else could be used
rules.bad: let v = if let Some(v) = w { v } else { return };

rules.description: Checks for references on std::path::MAIN_SEPARATOR.to_string() used to build a &str.
rules.bad: let s: &str = &std::path::MAIN_SEPARATOR.to_string();

rules.description: Checks for usage of match which could be implemented using map
Checks for for-loops that manually copy items between slices that could be optimized by having a memcpy.
Checks for manual implementation of midpoint.
Checks for .rev().next() on a DoubleEndedIterator
rules.bad: foo.iter().rev().next();

rules.description: Checks for manual implementations of the non-exhaustive pattern.
Checks for manual implementation of .ok() or .err() on Result values.
rules.good:
let a = func().ok();
let b = func().err();

rules.description: Finds patterns that reimplement Option::ok_or.
rules.bad:
let foo: Option<i32> = None;
foo.map_or(Err("error"), |v| Ok(v));

rules.description: This detects various manual reimplementations of Option::as_slice.
Checks for manual char comparison in string patterns
rules.bad: "Hello World!".trim_end_matches(|c| c == '.' || c == ',' || c == '!' || c == '?');

rules.description: Checks for expressions like x >= 3 && x < 8 that could be more readably expressed as (3..8).contains(x).
Looks for combined OR patterns that are all contained in a specific range, e.g. 6 | 4 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 8 can be rewritten as 4..=9.
Checks for an expression like ((x % 4) + 4) % 4 which is a common manual reimplementation of x.rem_euclid(4).
rules.good:
let x: i32 = 24;
let rem = x.rem_euclid(4);

rules.description: Checks for repeat().take() that can be replaced with repeat_n().
rules.bad: let _ = std::iter::repeat(10).take(3);

rules.description: Checks for code to be replaced by .retain().
It detects manual bit rotations that could be rewritten using standard functions rotate_left or rotate_right.
Checks for .checked_add/sub(x).unwrap_or(MAX/MIN).
rules.bad:
let add = x.checked_add(y).unwrap_or(u32::MAX);
let sub = x.checked_sub(y).unwrap_or(u32::MIN);

rules.description: Checks for manually filling a slice with a value.
When a is &[T], detect a.len() * size_of::<T>() and suggest size_of_val(a) instead.
rules.bad: let newlen = data.len() * size_of::<i32>();

rules.description: Checks for usage of str::splitn(2, _)
Checks for manual implementations of str::repeat
rules.bad: let x: String = std::iter::repeat('x').take(10).collect();

rules.description: Checks for usage of "" to create a String, such as "".to_string(), "".to_owned(), String::from("") and others.
rules.bad:
let a = "".to_string();
let b: String = "".into();
rules.good:
let a = String::new();
let b = String::new();

rules.description: Suggests using strip_{prefix,suffix} over str::{starts,ends}_with and slicing using the pattern’s length.
Checks for manual swapping. Note that the lint will not be emitted in const blocks, as the suggestion would not be applicable.
Detects manual re-implementations of std::mem::take.
rules.good:
let mut x = true;
let _ = std::mem::take(&mut x);

rules.description: Checks for usage of Iterator::fold with a type that implements Try.
rules.bad: vec![1, 2, 3].iter().fold(Some(0i32), |sum, i| sum?.checked_add(*i));

rules.description: Finds patterns that reimplement Option::unwrap_or or Result::unwrap_or.
Checks if a match or if let expression can be simplified using .unwrap_or_default().
Looks for loops that check for emptiness of a Vec in the condition and pop an element in the body as a separate operation.
Checks for too many variables whose name consists of a single character.
rules.bad: let (a, b, c, d, e, f, g) = (...);

rules.description: Checks for usage of .map(…), followed by .all(identity) or .any(identity).
rules.bad:
let e1 = v.iter().map(|s| s.is_empty()).all(|a| a);
let e2 = v.iter().map(|s| s.is_empty()).any(std::convert::identity);
rules.good:
let e1 = v.iter().all(|s| s.is_empty());
let e2 = v.iter().any(|s| s.is_empty());

rules.description: Checks for usage of map(|x| x.clone()) or dereferencing closures for Copy types, on Iterator or Option, and suggests cloned() or copied() instead
Checks for usage of _.map(_).collect::<Result<(), _>().
rules.bad: (0..3).map(|t| Err(t)).collect::<Result<(), _>>();

rules.description: Checks for usage of contains_key + insert on HashMap or BTreeMap.
Checks for instances of map_err(|_| Some::Enum)
Checks for usage of _.map(_).flatten(_) on Iterator and Option
Checks for instances of map(f) where f is the identity function.
rules.bad:
let x = [1, 2, 3];
let y: Vec<_> = x.iter().map(|x| x).map(|x| 2*x).collect();
rules.good:
let x = [1, 2, 3];
let y: Vec<_> = x.iter().map(|x| 2*x).collect();

rules.description: Checks for usage of option.map(_).unwrap_or(_) or option.map(_).unwrap_or_else(_) or result.map(_).unwrap_or_else(_).
Checks for Iterator::map over ranges without using the parameter which could be more clearly expressed using std::iter::repeat(...).take(...) or std::iter::repeat_n.
rules.bad: let random_numbers : Vec<_> = (0..10).map(|_| { 3 + 1 }).collect();

rules.description: Checks for match which is used to add a reference to an Option value.
rules.good:
let x: Option<()> = None;

let r: Option<&()> = x.as_ref();

rules.description: Checks for matches where match expression is a bool. It suggests to replace the expression with an if...else block.
Checks for match or if let expressions producing a bool that could be written using matches!
rules.good:
let x = Some(5);
let a = matches!(x, Some(0));

rules.description: Checks for overlapping match arms.
Checks for matches where all arms match a reference, suggesting to remove the reference and deref the matched expression instead. It also checks for if let &foo = bar blocks.
Checks for unnecessary ok() in while let.
Checks for match with identical arm bodies. Note: Does not lint on wildcards if the non_exhaustive_omitted_patterns_lint feature is enabled and disallowed.
Checks for useless match that binds to only one value.
Checks for match expressions modifying the case of a string with non-compliant arms
Checks for arm which matches all errors with Err(_) and take drastic actions like panic!.
Checks for wildcard enum matches for a single variant.
Checks for iteration that may be infinite.
rules.bad:
let infinite_iter = 0..;
[0..].iter().zip(infinite_iter.take_while(|x| *x > 5));

rules.description: Checks for usage of std::mem::forget(t) where t is Drop or has a field that implements Drop.
rules.bad: mem::forget(Rc::new(55))

rules.description: Checks for mem::replace() on an Option with None.
Checks for mem::replace() on an Option with Some(…).
rules.bad:
let mut an_option = Some(0);
let replaced = std::mem::replace(&mut an_option, Some(1));

rules.description: Checks for std::mem::replace on a value of type T with T::default().
rules.bad:
let mut text = String::from("foo");
let replaced = std::mem::replace(&mut text, String::default());

rules.description: Checks for mem::replace(&mut _, mem::uninitialized()) and mem::replace(&mut _, mem::zeroed()).
Checks for identifiers which consist of a single character (or fewer than the configured threshold). Note: This lint can be very noisy when enabled; it may be desirable to only enable it temporarily.
Checks for expressions where std::cmp::min and max are used to clamp values, but switched so that the result is constant.

Checks for type parameters which are positioned inconsistently between a type definition and impl block. Specifically, a parameter in an impl block which has the same name as a parameter in the type def, but is in a different place.
Checks for getter methods that return a field that doesn’t correspond to the name of the method, when there is a field’s whose name matches that of the method.
Checks for a op= a op b or a op= b op a patterns.
Checks assertions without a custom panic message.
Checks for repeated slice indexing without asserting beforehand that the length is greater than the largest index used to index into the slice.
Suggests the use of const in functions and methods where possible.
Suggests to use const in thread_local! macro if possible.

Checks for imports that do not rename the item as specified in the enforced-import-renames config option. Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if import renames are defined in the clippy.toml file.
Checks the doc comments of publicly visible functions that return a Result type and warns if there is no # Errors section.
Checks for manual core::fmt::Debug implementations that do not use all fields.
It lints if an exported function, method, trait method with default impl, or trait method impl is not #[inline].
Checks the doc comments of publicly visible functions that may panic and warns if there is no # Panics section.
Checks for the doc comments of publicly visible unsafe functions and warns if there is no # Safety section.
Checks for empty spin loops
Checks if a provided method is used implicitly by a trait implementation.
Checks if transmute calls have all generics specified.
Warns for mistyped suffix in literals
Checks for items that have the same kind of attributes with mixed styles (inner/outer).
Warns on hexadecimal literals with mixed-case letter digits.
rules.bad: 0x1a9BAcD

rules.description: Checks for a read and a write to the same variable where whether the read occurs before or after the write depends on the evaluation order of sub-expressions.
Checks that module layout uses only self named module files; bans mod.rs files.
rules.bad:
src/
  stuff/
    stuff_files.rs
    mod.rs
  lib.rs

rules.description: Checks for modules that have the same name as their parent module
Detects public item names that are prefixed or suffixed by the containing public module’s name.
Checks for modulo arithmetic.
Checks for getting the remainder of integer division by one or minus one.
rules.bad:
let a = x % 1;
let a = x % -1;

rules.description: Checks for nested assignments.
Check if a generic is defined both in the bound predicate and in the where clause.
Checks to see if multiple versions of a crate are being used.
rules.bad:
[dependencies]
ctrlc = "=3.1.0"
ansi_term = "=0.11.0"

rules.description: Checks for multiple inherent implementations of a struct The config option controls the scope in which multiple inherent impl blocks for the same struct are linted, allowing values of module (only within the same module), file (within the s
Checks for unsafe blocks that contain more than one unsafe operation.
Checks for public functions that have no #[must_use] attribute, but return something not already marked must-use, have no mutable arg and mutate no statics.
rules.bad:
// this could be annotated with `#[must_use]`.
pub fn id<T>(t: T) -> T { t }

rules.description: Checks for a #[must_use] attribute on unit-returning functions and methods.
rules.bad:
#[must_use]
fn useless() { }

rules.description: This lint checks for functions that take immutable references and return mutable ones. This will not trigger if no unsafe code exists as there are multiple safe functions which will do this transformation To be on the conservative side, if
rules.bad: fn foo(&Foo) -> &mut Bar { .. }

rules.description: Checks for instances of mut mut references.
Checks for &mut Mutex::lock calls
Checks for loops with a range bound that is a mutable variable.
Checks for sets/maps with mutable key types.
Checks for usage of Mutex<X> where an atomic will do.
rules.bad: let x = Mutex::new(&y);

rules.description: Checks for usage of Mutex<X> where X is an integral type.
rules.bad: let x = Mutex::new(0usize);

rules.description: Checks for naive byte counts
rules.bad: let count = vec.iter().filter(|x| **x == 0u8).count();

rules.description: The lint checks for self in fn parameters that specify the Self-type explicitly
It detects useless calls to str::as_bytes() before calling len() or is_empty().
rules.bad:
let len = "some string".as_bytes().len();
let b = "some string".as_bytes().is_empty();
rules.good:
let len = "some string".len();
let b = "some string".is_empty();

rules.description: Checks for usage of bitwise and/or operators between booleans, where performance may be improved by using a lazy and.
rules.bad:
let (x,y) = (true, false);
if x & !y {} // where both x and y are booleans

rules.description: Checks for expressions of the form if c { true } else { false } (or vice versa) and suggests using the condition directly.
Checks for expressions of the form if c { x = true } else { x = false } (or vice versa) and suggest assigning the variable directly from the condition.
Checks for address of operations (&) that are going to be dereferenced immediately by the compiler.
Checks for bindings that needlessly destructure a reference and borrow the inner value with &ref.
Checks for borrow operations (&) that are used as a generic argument to a function when the borrowed value could be used.
Checks if an iterator is used to check if a string is ascii.
rules.bad: "foo".chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii());

rules.description: Checks for functions collecting an iterator when collect is not needed.
rules.bad: let len = iterator.collect::<Vec<_>>().len();

rules.description: The lint checks for if-statements appearing in loops that contain a continue statement in either their main blocks or their else-blocks, when omitting the else-block possibly with some rearrangement of code can make the code easier to under
Checks for fn main() { .. } in doctests
Checks for empty else branches.
Checks for usage of for_each that would be more simply written as a for loop.
Checks for empty if branches with no else branch.
rules.bad:
if really_expensive_condition(&i) {}
if really_expensive_condition_with_side_effects(&mut i) {}

rules.description: Checks for late initializations that can be replaced by a let statement with an initializer.
Checks for lifetime annotations which can be removed by relying on lifetime elision.
Checks for unnecessary match or match-like if let returns for Option and Result when function signatures are the same.
Lints ?Sized bounds applied to type parameters that cannot be unsized
rules.bad:
// `T` cannot be unsized because `Clone` requires it to be `Sized`
fn f<T: Clone + ?Sized>(t: &T) {}
rules.good:
fn f<T: Clone>(t: &T) {}

// or choose alternative bounds for `T` so that it can be unsized

rules.description: Checks for no-op uses of Option::{as_deref, as_deref_mut}, for example, Option<&T>::as_deref() returns the same type.
rules.bad:
let a = Some(&1);
let b = a.as_deref(); // goes from Option<&i32> to Option<&i32>
rules.good:
let a = Some(&1);
let b = a;

rules.description: Checks for calling take function after as_ref.
rules.bad:
let x = Some(3);
x.as_ref().take();

rules.description: The lint checks for parenthesis on literals in range statements that are superfluous.

Check if a &mut function argument is actually used mutably. Be careful if the function is publicly reexported as it would break compatibility with users of this function, when the users pass this function as an argument.
Checks for functions taking arguments by value, but not consuming them in its body.
Checks for usage of pub(self) and pub(in self).
rules.bad: pub(self) type OptBox<T> = Option<Box<T>>;

rules.description: Suggests replacing Ok(x?) or Some(x?) with x in return positions where the ? operator is not needed to convert the type of x.
Checks for looping over the range of 0..len of some collection just to get the values by index.
Checks for raw string literals with an unnecessary amount of hashes around them.
rules.bad: let r = r###"Hello, "world"!"###;

rules.description: Checks for raw string literals where a string literal can be used instead.
rules.bad: let r = r"Hello, world!";

rules.description: Checks for return statements at the end of a block.
Checks for return statements on Err paired with the ? operator.
Checks for usage of str::splitn (or str::rsplitn) where using str::split would be the same.
rules.bad:
let str = "key=value=add";
let _ = str.splitn(3, '=').next().unwrap();

rules.description: Checks for bindings (constants, statics, or let bindings) that are defined with one numeric type but are consistently cast to a different type in all usages.
rules.bad:
const SIZE: u16 = 15;
let arr: [u8; SIZE as usize] = [0; SIZE as usize];
rules.good:
const SIZE: usize = 15;
let arr: [u8; SIZE] = [0; SIZE];

rules.description: Checks for needlessly including a base struct on update when all fields are changed anyway. This lint is not applied to structs marked with non_exhaustive.
Checks for the usage of negated comparison operators on types which only implement PartialOrd (e.g., f64).
Checks for multiplication by -1 as a form of negation.
rules.bad: let a = x * -1;

rules.description: Checks for negative feature names with prefix no- or not-
rules.bad:
[features]
default = []
no-abc = []
not-def = []

rules.description: Checks for loops that will always break, return or continue an outer loop.
Checks for new not returning a type that contains Self.
Checks for public types with a pub fn new() -> Self method and no implementation of Default.
Checks for replace statements which have no effect.
Checks for binding to underscore prefixed variable without side-effects.
rules.bad: let _i_serve_no_purpose = 1;

rules.description: Checks for Rust ABI functions with the #[no_mangle] attribute.
rules.bad:
#[no_mangle]
 fn example(arg_one: u32, arg_two: usize) {}
rules.good:
#[no_mangle]
 extern "C" fn example(arg_one: u32, arg_two: usize) {}

rules.description: Checks for non-ASCII characters in string and char literals.
rules.bad: let x = String::from("€");

rules.description: Checks for non-canonical implementations of Clone when Copy is already implemented.
Checks for non-canonical implementations of PartialOrd when Ord is already implemented.
Checks for any and all combinators in cfg with only one condition.
rules.bad:
#[cfg(any(unix))]
pub struct Bar;

rules.description: Checks for non-octal values used to set Unix file permissions.

This lint warns about a Send implementation for a type that contains fields that are not safe to be sent across threads. It tries to detect fields that can cause a soundness issue when sent to another thread (e.g., Rc) while allowing !Send

Lints when once_cell::sync::Lazy or lazy_static! are used to define a static variable, and suggests replacing such cases with std::sync::LazyLock instead. Note: This lint will not trigger in crate with no_std context, or with MSRV < 1.80.0.
rules.good:
static FOO: std::sync::LazyLock<String> = std::sync::LazyLock::new(|| "FOO".to_lowercase());
static BAR: std::sync::LazyLock<String> = std::sync::LazyLock::new(|| "BAR".to_lowercase());

rules.description: Checks for conversions from NonZero types to regular integer types, and suggests using NonZero types for the target as well.
Checks for boolean expressions that can be written more concisely.
rules.bad:
if a && true {}
if !(a == b) {}

rules.description: Checks for duplicate open options as well as combinations that make no sense.
rules.bad:
use std::fs::OpenOptions;

OpenOptions::new().read(true).truncate(true);

rules.description: Checks that common macros are used with consistent bracing.
Checks for public functions that dereference raw pointer arguments but are not marked unsafe.
Checks for unnecessary method chains that can be simplified into if .. else ...
rules.bad:
let x = true;
x.then_some("a").unwrap_or("b");
rules.good:
let x = true;
if x { "a" } else { "b" };

rules.description: Checks for \0 escapes in string and byte literals that look like octal character escapes in C.
rules.bad:
let one = "\033[1m Bold? \033[0m";  // \033 intended as escape
let two = "\033\0";                 // \033 intended as null-3-3
rules.good:
let one = "\x1b[1mWill this be bold?\x1b[0m";
let two = "\x0033\x00";

rules.description: Checks for usage of ok().expect(..). Note: This lint only triggers for code marked compatible with versions of the compiler older than Rust 1.82.0.
rules.bad: x.ok().expect("why did I do this again?");
rules.good: x.expect("why did I do this again?");

rules.description: Checks for arguments that are only used in recursion with no side-effects.
Checks for arguments to == which have their address taken to satisfy a bound and suggests to dereference the other argument instead
rules.bad: &x == y

rules.description: Checks for usage of .as_ref().cloned() and .as_mut().cloned() on Options
Checks for usage of _.as_ref().map(Deref::deref) or its aliases (such as String::as_str).
rules.bad: opt.as_ref().map(String::as_str)

rules.description: Checks for usage of option_env!(...).unwrap() and suggests usage of the env! macro.
rules.bad: let _ = option_env!("HOME").unwrap();

rules.description: Checks for iterators of Options using .filter(Option::is_some).map(Option::unwrap) that may be replaced with a .flatten() call.
rules.bad: let _ = std::iter::empty::<Option<i32>>().filter(Option::is_some).map(Option::unwrap);

rules.description: Lints usage of if let Some(v) = ... { y } else { x } and match .. { Some(v) => y, None/_ => x } which are more idiomatically done with Option::map_or (if the else bit is a pure expression) or Option::map_or_else (if the else bit is an impur
Checks for usage of _.map_or(None, _).
rules.bad: opt.map_or(None, |a| Some(a + 1));

rules.description: Checks for usage of option.map(f) where f is a function or closure that returns the unit type ().
Checks for usage of Option<Option<_>> in function signatures and type definitions

Checks for calls to .or(foo(..)), .unwrap_or(foo(..)), .or_insert(foo(..)) etc., and suggests to use .or_else(|| foo(..)), .unwrap_or_else(|| foo(..)), .unwrap_or_default() or .or_default() etc. instead.
rules.bad: foo.unwrap_or(String::from("empty"));

rules.description: Checks for .or(…).unwrap() calls to Options and Results.
Checks for out of bounds array indexing with a constant index.
Checks for boolean expressions that contain terminals that can be eliminated.
rules.bad:
// The `b` is unnecessary, the expression is equivalent to `if a`.
if a && b || a { ... }

rules.description: Detects needlessly owned Cow types.
rules.bad: let wrogn: std::borrow::Cow<'_, Vec<u8>>;

rules.description: Checks for usage of panic!.
rules.bad: panic!("even with a good reason");

rules.description: Checks for usage of panic! or assertions in a function whose return type is Result.
Detects C-style underflow/overflow checks.
Checks for calls of unwrap[_err]() that will always fail.
Checks whether some but not all fields of a struct are public. Either make all fields of a type public, or make none of them public
Checks for manual re-implementations of PartialEq::ne.
Checks for binary comparisons to a literal Option::None.
Checks for push calls on PathBuf that can cause overwrites.

Looks for calls to Path::ends_with calls where the argument looks like a file extension. By default, Clippy has a short list of known filenames that start with a dot but aren’t necessarily file extensions (e.g. the .git folder), which are a
Checks for calls to push immediately after creating a new PathBuf.
rules.bad:
let mut path_buf = PathBuf::new();
path_buf.push("foo");

rules.description: Checks for patterns that aren’t exact representations of the types they are applied to. To satisfy this lint, you will have to adjust either the expression that is matched against or the pattern itself, as well as the bindings that are intr
Checks for calls to std::fs::Permissions.set_readonly with argument false.
Detects pointer format as well as Debug formatting of raw pointers or function pointers or any types that have a derived Debug impl that recursively contains them.
Checks if any pointer is being passed to an asm! block with nomem option.
Checks for possible missing comma in an array. It lints if an array element is a binary operator expression and it lies on two lines.
Checks for an if expression followed by either a block or another if that looks like it should have an else between them.
Checks for usage of println, print, eprintln or eprint in an implementation of a formatting trait.
This lint warns about the use of literals as print!/println! args.
rules.bad: println!("{}", "foo");

rules.description: Checks for printing on stderr. The purpose of this lint is to catch debugging remnants.
rules.bad: eprintln!("Hello world!");

rules.description: Checks for printing on stdout. The purpose of this lint is to catch debugging remnants.
rules.bad: println!("Hello world!");

rules.description: This lint warns when you use print!() with a format string that ends in a newline.
rules.bad: print!("Hello {}!\n", name);

rules.description: This lint warns when you use println!("") to print a newline.
rules.bad: println!("");

rules.description: This lint checks for function arguments of type &String, &Vec, &PathBuf, and Cow<_>. It will also suggest you replace .clone() calls with the appropriate .to_owned()/to_string() calls.
rules.bad: fn foo(&Vec<u32>) { .. }

rules.description: Checks for as casts between raw pointers that don’t change their constness, namely *const T to *const U and *mut T to *mut U.
Checks for as casts between raw pointers that change their constness, namely *const T to *mut T and *mut T to *const T.
Use std::ptr::eq when applicable
Checks for usage of the offset pointer method with an integer literal.
Checks for usage of the offset pointer method with a usize casted to an isize.
Checks whether any field of the struct is prefixed with an _ (underscore) and also marked pub (public)
Restricts the usage of pub use ...
Checks for usage of pub(<loc>) with in.
rules.bad: pub(super) type OptBox<T> = Option<Box<T>>;

rules.description: Checks for usage of pub(<loc>) without in. Note: As you cannot write a module’s path in pub(<loc>), this will only trigger on pub(super) and the like.
rules.bad: pub(in super) type OptBox<T> = Option<Box<T>>;

rules.description: Checks for expressions that could be replaced by the ? operator.
Checks for expressions that use the ? operator and rejects them.
rules.bad: let result = expr?;

rules.description: Checks for inclusive ranges where 1 is subtracted from the upper bound, e.g., x..=(y-1).
Checks for exclusive ranges where 1 is added to the upper bound, e.g., x..(y+1).
Checks for zipping a collection with the range of 0.._.len().
rules.bad: let _ = x.iter().zip(0..x.len());

rules.description: Checks for Rc<T> and Arc<T> when T is a mutable buffer type such as String or Vec.
rules.bad: fn foo(interned: Rc<String>) { ... }

rules.description: Checks for reference-counted pointers (Arc, Rc, rc::Weak, and sync::Weak) in vec![elem; len]
Checks for Rc<Mutex<T>>.

Looks for calls to [Stdin::read_line] to read a line from the standard input into a string, then later attempting to use that string for an operation that will never work for strings with a trailing newline character in it (e.g. parsing int
This lint catches reads into a zero-length Vec. Especially in the case of a call to with_capacity, this lint warns that read gets the number of bytes from the Vec’s length, not its capacity.
Looks for calls to RwLock::write where the lock is only used for reading.
Checks for format trait implementations (e.g. Display) with a recursive call to itself which uses self as a parameter. This is typically done indirectly with the write! macro or with to_string().
Checks for usage of redundant allocations anywhere in the code.
rules.bad: fn foo(bar: Rc<&usize>) {}

rules.description: Checks for usage of as_str() on a String chained with a method available on the String itself.
rules.bad:
let owned_string = "This is a string".to_owned();
owned_string.as_str().as_bytes()
rules.good:
let owned_string = "This is a string".to_owned();
owned_string.as_bytes()

rules.description: Checks for async block that only returns await on a future.
Checks for [all @ ..] patterns.
Checks for a redundant clone() (and its relatives) which clones an owned value that is going to be dropped without further use.

Checks for closures which just call another function where the function can be called directly. unsafe functions, calls where types get adjusted or where the callee is marked #[track_caller] are ignored.
rules.bad: xs.map(|x| foo(x))
rules.good:
// where `foo(_)` is a plain function that takes the exact argument type of `x`.
xs.map(foo)

rules.description: Detects closures called in the same expression where they are defined.
Checks for closures which only invoke a method on the closure argument and can be replaced by referencing the method directly.
rules.bad: Some('a').map(|s| s.to_uppercase());

rules.description: Checks for ineffective double comparisons against constants.
rules.bad: if status_code <= 400 && status_code < 500 {}

rules.description: Checks for else blocks that can be removed without changing semantics.
Checks for feature names with prefix use-, with- or suffix -support
rules.bad:
[features]
default = ["use-abc", "with-def", "ghi-support"]
use-abc = []  // redundant
with-def = []   // redundant
ghi-support = []   // redundant

rules.description: Checks for fields in struct literals where shorthands could be used.
Checks for unnecessary guards in match expressions.
Checks for calls to Iterator::cloned where the original value could be used instead.
rules.bad:
let x = vec![String::new()];
let _ = x.iter().cloned().map(|x| x.len());
rules.good:
let x = vec![String::new()];
let _ = x.iter().map(|x| x.len());

rules.description: Checks for redundant redefinitions of local bindings.
Checks for patterns in the form name @ _.
Lint for redundant pattern matching over Result, Option, std::task::Poll, std::net::IpAddr or bools
Checks for items declared pub(crate) that are not crate visible because they are inside a private module.
Checks for redundant slicing expressions which use the full range, and do not change the type.
Checks for constants and statics with an explicit 'static lifetime.
rules.bad:
const FOO: &'static [(&'static str, &'static str, fn(&Bar) -> bool)] =
&[...]
static FOO: &'static [(&'static str, &'static str, fn(&Bar) -> bool)] =
&[...]

rules.description: Checks for test functions (functions annotated with #[test]) that are prefixed with test_ which is redundant.
Warns about needless / redundant type annotations.
rules.bad: let foo: String = String::new();

rules.description: Checks for casts of references to pointer using as and suggests std::ptr::from_ref and std::ptr::from_mut instead.
rules.bad:
let a_ref = &1;
let a_ptr = a_ref as *const _;

rules.description: Checks for ref bindings which create a reference to a reference.
Warns when a function signature uses &Option<T> instead of Option<&T>.
Checks for usage of &Option<&T>.
rules.bad: let x: &Option<&u32> = &Some(&0u32);

rules.description: Checks for usages of the ref keyword.
rules.bad:
let opt = Some(5);
if let Some(ref foo) = opt {}
rules.good:
let opt = Some(5);
if let Some(foo) = &opt {}

rules.description: Checks for regex compilation inside a loop with a literal.
Lints when the name of function parameters from trait impl is different than its default implementation.

Checks for usage of .repeat(1) and suggest the following method for each types. .to_string() for str .clone() for String .to_vec() for slice The lint will evaluate constant expressions and values as arguments of .repeat(..) and emit a messa
Looks for patterns such as vec![Vec::with_capacity(x); n] or iter::repeat(Vec::with_capacity(x)).
rules.bad:
let _: Vec<Vec<u8>> = vec![Vec::with_capacity(42); 123];
let _: Vec<Vec<u8>> = iter::repeat(Vec::with_capacity(42)).take(123).collect();

rules.description: Detects assignments of Default::default() or Box::new(value) to a place of type Box<T>.
rules.bad:
let mut b = Box::new(1u32);
b = Default::default();

rules.description: Checks for items with #[repr(packed)]-attribute without ABI qualification
Informs the user about a more concise way to create a vector with a known capacity.
rules.bad:
let mut v: Vec<usize> = vec![];
v.reserve(10);

rules.description: Checks for unnecessary ‘..’ pattern binding on struct when all fields are explicitly matched.
Checks for iterators of Results using .filter(Result::is_ok).map(Result::unwrap) that may be replaced with a .flatten() call.
rules.bad: let _ = std::iter::empty::<Result<i32, ()>>().filter(Result::is_ok).map(Result::unwrap);

rules.description: Checks for functions that return Result with an unusually large Err-variant.
Checks for usage of _.map_or(None, Some).
rules.bad: assert_eq!(Some(1), r.map_or(None, Some));

rules.description: Checks for usage of result.map(f) where f is a function or closure that returns the unit type ().
Checks for public functions that return a Result with an Err type of (). It suggests using a custom type that implements std::error::Error.
