rules.bad: pub fn read_u8() -> Result<u8, ()> { Err(()) }

rules.description: Detect functions that end with Option::and_then or Result::and_then, and suggest using the ? operator instead.
This lint warns when a method returning Self doesn’t have the #[must_use] attribute.
Checks for range expressions x..y where both x and y are constant and x is greater to y. Also triggers if x is equal to y when they are conditions to a for loop.
Checks for consecutive ifs with the same function call.
Checks whether a for loop is being used to push a constant value into a Vec.
Checks for usages of Vec::from_raw_parts and String::from_raw_parts where the same expression is used for the length and the capacity.
It lints if a struct has two methods with the same name: one from a trait, another not from a trait.
Checks for an iterator or string search (such as find(), position(), or rposition()) followed by a call to is_some() or is_none().
Checks if the seek method of the Seek trait is called with SeekFrom::Current(0), and if it is, suggests using stream_position instead.
Checks for jumps to the start of a stream that implements Seek and uses the seek method providing Start as parameter.
Checks for explicit self-assignments.
Warns when constructors have the same name as their types.
Checks that module layout uses only mod.rs files.
rules.bad:
src/
  stuff/
    stuff_files.rs
  stuff.rs
  lib.rs

rules.description: Checks for self receiver that is only used in recursion with no side-effects.
Looks for blocks of expressions and fires if the last expression returns () but is not followed by a semicolon.
Suggests moving the semicolon after a block to the inside of the block, after its last expression.
rules.bad: unsafe { f(x) };

rules.description: Suggests moving the semicolon from a block’s final expression outside of the block.
rules.bad: unsafe { f(x); }

rules.description: Warns if literal suffixes are separated by an underscore. To enforce separated literal suffix style, see the unseparated_literal_suffix lint.
Checks for usage of contains to see if a value is not present in a set like HashSet or BTreeSet, followed by an insert.
Checks for bindings that shadow other bindings already in scope, while reusing the original value.
rules.bad:
let x = 2;
let x = x + 1;

rules.description: Checks for bindings that shadow other bindings already in scope, while just changing reference level or mutability.
rules.bad: let x = &x;
rules.good: let y = &x; // use different variable name

rules.description: Checks for bindings that shadow other bindings already in scope, either without an initialization or with one that does not even use the original value.
rules.bad:
let x = y;
let x = z; // shadows the earlier binding
rules.good:
let x = y;
let w = z; // use different variable name

rules.description: Checks for the use of short circuit boolean conditions as a statement.
rules.bad: f() && g(); // We should write `if f() { g(); }`.

rules.description: Checks for methods that should live in a trait implementation of a std trait (see llogiq’s blog post for further information) instead of an inherent implementation.
Checks for #[should_panic] attributes without specifying the expected panic message.
Checks for temporaries returned from function calls in a match scrutinee that have the clippy::has_significant_drop attribute.

Searches for elements marked with #[clippy::has_significant_drop] that could be early dropped but are in fact dropped at the end of their scopes. In other words, enforces the “tightening” of their possible lifetimes.

Checks for names that are very similar and thus confusing. Note: this lint looks for similar names throughout each scope. To allow it, you need to allow it on the scope level, not on the name that is reported.
rules.bad:
let checked_exp = something;
let checked_expr = something_else;

rules.description: Checks for functions that are only used once. Does not lint tests.
Warns when using push_str/insert_str with a single-character string literal where push/insert with a char would work fine.
rules.bad:
string.insert_str(0, "R");
string.push_str("R");

rules.description: Checks for lifetimes with names which are one character long.
Checks for string methods that receive a single-character str as an argument, e.g., _.split("x").
rules.bad: _.split("x");

rules.description: Checking for imports with single component use path.
Checks whether a for loop has a single element.

Checks for matches with a single arm where an if let will usually suffice. This intentionally does not lint if there are comments inside of the other arm, so as to allow the user to document why having another explicit pattern with an empty
Checks for matches with two arms where an if let else will usually suffice.
Checks for functions with method calls to .map(_) on an arg of type Option as the outermost expression.
Checks for Vec or array initializations that contain only one range.
Detects expressions where size_of::<T> or size_of_val::<T> is used as a count of elements of type T
Checks for calls to size_of_val() where the argument is a reference to a reference.
Checks for usage of _.skip_while(condition).next().
rules.bad: vec.iter().skip_while(|x| **x == 0).next();

rules.description: Checks for string slices immediately followed by as_bytes.
rules.bad:
let s = "Lorem ipsum";
s[1..5].as_bytes();

rules.description: Checks slow zero-filled vector initialization
When sorting primitive values (integers, bools, chars, as well as arrays, slices, and tuples of such items), it is typically better to use an unstable sort than a stable sort.
rules.bad:
let mut vec = vec![2, 1, 3];
vec.sort();

rules.description: Finds items imported through std when available through alloc.
rules.bad: use std::vec::Vec;

rules.description: Finds items imported through std when available through core.
rules.bad: use std::hash::Hasher;

rules.description: Checks for usages of str.trim().split("\n") and str.trim().split("\r\n").
rules.bad:
"some
text
with
newlines
".trim().split('
');

rules.description: This lint checks for .to_string() method calls on values of type &str.
rules.bad: let _ = "str".to_string();

rules.description: Checks for all instances of x + _ where x is of type String, but only if string_add_assign does not match.
rules.bad:
let x = "Hello".to_owned();
x + ", World";

rules.description: Checks for string appends of the form x = x + y (without let!).
Checks for the use of .extend(s.chars()) where s is a &str or String.
Check if the string is transformed to byte array and casted back to string.
rules.bad: std::str::from_utf8(&"Hello World!".as_bytes()[6..11]).unwrap();

rules.description: Checks for the as_bytes method called on string literals that contain only ASCII characters.
Checks for <string_lit>.chars().any(|i| i == c).
Checks for slice operations on strings
rules.bad: &"Ölkanne"[1..];

rules.description: Checks for usage of libc::strlen on a CString or CStr value, and suggest calling count_bytes() instead.
Checks for excessive use of bools in structs.
Detects struct fields that are prefixed or suffixed by the same characters or the name of the struct itself.
Looks for floating-point expressions that can be expressed using built-in methods to improve both accuracy and performance.
Lints for suspicious operations in impls of arithmetic operators, e.g. subtracting elements in an Add impl.
Checks for usage of the non-existent =*, =! and =- operators.
rules.bad: a =- 42; // confusing, should it be `a -= 42` or `a = -42`?

rules.description: Checks for Command::arg() invocations that look like they should be multiple arguments instead, such as arg("-t ext2").
rules.bad: std::process::Command::new("echo").arg("-n hello").spawn().unwrap();

rules.description: Detects the use of outer doc comments (///, /**) followed by a bang (!): ///!
Checks for formatting of else. It lints if the else is followed immediately by a newline or the else seems to be missing.
Checks for calls to map followed by a count.
rules.bad: let _ = (0..3).map(|x| x + 2).count();

rules.description: Lints for suspicious operations in impls of OpAssign, e.g. subtracting elements in an AddAssign impl.
Checks for the suspicious use of OpenOptions::create() without an explicit OpenOptions::truncate().
rules.bad:
use std::fs::OpenOptions;

OpenOptions::new().create(true);

rules.description: Checks for unlikely usages of binary operators that are almost certainly typos and/or copy/paste errors, given the other usages of binary operators nearby.
Checks for calls to [splitn] (https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.splitn) and related functions with either zero or one splits.
Checks for the usage of _.to_owned(), on a Cow<'_, _>.

Checks the formatting of a unary operator on the right hand side of a binary operator. It lints if there is no space between the binary and unary operators, but there is a space between the unary and its operand.
rules.bad:
// &&! looks like a different operator
if foo &&! bar {}

rules.description: Warns for a Bitwise XOR (^) operator being probably confused as a powering. It will not trigger if any of the numbers are not in decimal.
Checks for calls to core::mem::swap where either parameter is derived from a pointer
Checks for usage of std::mem::swap with temporary values.
Checks doc comments for usage of tab characters.
Checks for construction of a structure or tuple just to assign a value in it.
Checks for #[test] in doctests unless they are marked with either ignore, no_run or compile_fail.
Triggers when a testing function (marked with the #[test] attribute) isn’t inside a testing module (marked with #[cfg(test)]).
Checks for .to_digit(..).is_some() on chars.
rules.bad: let is_digit = c.to_digit(radix).is_some();

rules.description: Checks for ToString::to_string applied to a type that implements Display in a macro that does formatting.
rules.bad: println!("error: something failed at {}", Location::caller().to_string());

rules.description: Checks for direct implementations of ToString.
Checks for usage of todo!.
rules.bad: todo!();

rules.description: Checks if the first paragraph in the documentation of items listed in the module page is too long.
Checks for functions with too many parameters.
Checks for functions with a large amount of lines.
Checks for function arguments and let bindings denoted as ref.
rules.bad: fn foo(ref _x: u8) {}

rules.description: Displays a warning when a struct with a trailing zero-sized array is declared without a repr attribute.
Checks for cases where generics or trait objects are being used and multiple syntax specifications for trait bounds are used simultaneously.
rules.bad: fn func<T: Clone + Default>(arg: T) where T: Clone + Default {}

rules.description: Checks for transmutes from a &[u8] to a &str.
Checks for transmutes from an integer to a bool.
Checks for transmutes from T to NonZero<T>, and suggests the new_unchecked method instead.
rules.bad: let _: NonZero<u32> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(123) };

rules.description: Checks for null function pointer creation through transmute.
rules.bad: let null_fn: fn() = unsafe { std::mem::transmute( std::ptr::null::<()>() ) };

rules.description: Checks for transmutes from a pointer to a pointer, or from a reference to a reference.
Checks for transmutes from a pointer to a reference.
Checks for transmutes between types which do not have a representation defined relative to each other.
rules.bad:
struct Foo<T>(u32, T);
let _ = unsafe { core::mem::transmute::<Foo<u32>, Foo<i32>>(Foo(0u32, 0u32)) };

rules.description: Checks for transmutes that could be a pointer cast.
rules.bad: unsafe { std::mem::transmute::<*const [i32], *const [u16]>(p) };

rules.description: Checks for transmute calls which would receive a null pointer.
rules.bad: let null_ref: &u64 = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(0 as *const u64) };

rules.description: Warns about calling str::trim (or variants) before str::split_whitespace.
rules.bad: " A B C ".trim().split_whitespace();

rules.description: Checks for trivial regex creation (with Regex::new, RegexBuilder::new, or RegexSet::new).
rules.bad: Regex::new("^foobar")

rules.description: Checks for functions taking arguments by reference, where the argument type is Copy and small enough to be more efficient to always pass by value.
rules.bad: fn foo(v: &u32) {}

rules.description: Checks for usage of Err(x)?.
Checks for tuple<=>array conversions that are not done with .into().
rules.good:
let t1 = &[(1, 2), (3, 4)];
let v1: Vec<[u32; 2]> = t1.iter().map(|&t| t.into()).collect();

rules.description: Checks for types used in structs, parameters and let declarations above a certain complexity threshold.
Looks for calls to .type_id() on a Box<dyn _>.
This lint warns about unnecessary type repetitions in trait bounds
rules.bad: pub fn foo<T>(t: T) where T: Copy, T: Clone {}
rules.good: pub fn foo<T>(t: T) where T: Copy + Clone {}

rules.description: Checks for calls to Read::bytes on types which don’t implement BufRead.
Lints subtraction between an Instant and a Duration, or between two Duration values.
Checks that there isn’t an infinite recursion in trait implementations.

Checks for unsafe blocks and impls without a // SAFETY: comment explaining why the unsafe operations performed inside the block are safe. Note the comment must appear on the line(s) preceding the unsafe block with nothing appearing in betwe
Checks for usage of unimplemented!.
rules.bad: unimplemented!();

rules.description: It detects references to uninhabited types, such as ! and warns when those are either dereferenced or returned from a function.
Checks for MaybeUninit::uninit().assume_init().

Checks for set_len() call that creates Vec with uninitialized elements. This is commonly caused by calling set_len() right after allocating or reserving a buffer with new(), default(), with_capacity(), or reserve().
Detect when a variable is not inlined in a format string, and suggests to inline it.
Checks for passing a unit value as an argument to a function without using a unit literal (()).
Checks for comparisons to unit. This includes all binary comparisons (like == and <) and asserts.
Detects ().hash(_).

Checks for functions that expect closures of type Fn(…) -> Ord where the implemented closure returns the unit type. The lint also suggests to remove the semi-colon at the end of the statement if present.
rules.bad:
let mut twins = vec![(1, 1), (2, 2)];
twins.sort_by_key(|x| { x.1; });

rules.description: Checks for a return type containing a Box<T> where T implements Sized The lint ignores Box<T> where T is larger than unnecessary_box_size, as returning a large T directly may be detrimental to performance.
Checks for casts to the same type, casts of int literals to integer types, casts of float literals to float types, and casts between raw pointers that don’t change type or constness.
rules.bad:
let _ = 2i32 as i32;
let _ = 0.5 as f32;

rules.description: Checks for #[cfg_attr(clippy, allow(clippy::lint))] and suggests to replace it with #[allow(clippy::lint)].
rules.bad: #![cfg_attr(clippy, allow(clippy::deprecated_cfg_attr))]

rules.description: Checks for Debug formatting ({:?}) applied to an OsStr or Path.
rules.bad:
let path = Path::new("...");
println!("The path is {:?}", path);
rules.good:
let path = Path::new("…");
println!("The path is {}", path.display());

rules.description: Checks for calls to TryInto::try_into and TryFrom::try_from when their infallible counterparts could be used.
rules.bad:
let _: Result<i64, _> = 1i32.try_into();
let _: Result<i64, _> = <_>::try_from(1i32);

rules.description: Checks for filter_map calls that could be replaced by filter or map. More specifically it checks if the closure provided is only performing one of the filter or map operations and suggests the appropriate option.

Checks for find_map calls that could be replaced by find or map. More specifically it checks if the closure provided is only performing one of the find or map operations and suggests the appropriate option.
Checks the usage of .first().is_some() or .first().is_none() to check if a slice is empty.
Checks for usage of fold when a more succinct alternative exists. Specifically, this checks for folds which could be replaced by any, all, sum or product.
rules.bad: (0..3).fold(false, |acc, x| acc || x > 2);

rules.description: Checks the usage of .get().is_some() or .get().is_none() on std map types.
Checks for usage of .collect::<Vec<String>>().join("") on iterators.

As the counterpart to or_fun_call, this lint looks for unnecessary lazily evaluated closures on Option and Result. This lint suggests changing the following functions, when eager evaluation results in simpler code: unwrap_or_else to unwrap_
rules.bad:
let opt: Option<u32> = None;

opt.unwrap_or_else(|| 42);

rules.description: Detects functions that are written to return &str that could return &'static str but instead return a &'a str.
Checks for .unwrap() related calls on Results and Options that are constructed.
Suggests removing the use of a map() (or map_err()) method when an Option or Result is being constructed.
rules.bad: Some(4).map(i32::swap_bytes)

rules.description: Converts some constructs mapping an Enum value for equality comparison.
Checks for unnecessary calls to min() or max() in the following cases Either both side is constant One side is clearly larger than the other, like i32::MIN and an i32 variable
Detects passing a mutable reference to a function that only requires an immutable reference.
rules.bad: vec.push(&mut value);

rules.description: Checks for expression statements that can be reduced to a sub-expression.
rules.bad: compute_array()[0];

rules.description: Detects cases of owned empty strings being passed as an argument to a function expecting &str
rules.bad: vec!["1", "2", "3"].join(&String::new());

rules.description: Checks for usage of .map_or_else() “map closure” for Result type.
rules.bad:
let x: Result<u32, ()> = Ok(0);
let y = x.map_or_else(|err| handle_error(err), |n| n);
rules.good:
let x: Result<u32, ()> = Ok(0);
let y = x.unwrap_or_else(|err| handle_error(err));

rules.description: Checks for // SAFETY: comments on safe code.
Checks for the doc comments of publicly visible safe functions and traits and warns if there is a # Safety section.
Checks for imports ending in ::{self}.
rules.bad: use std::io::{self};

rules.description: Checks for the presence of a semicolon at the end of a match or if statement evaluating to ().
Checks for usage of Vec::sort_by passing in a closure which compares the two arguments, either directly or indirectly.
rules.bad: vec.sort_by(|a, b| a.foo().cmp(&b.foo()));

rules.description: Checks for initialization of an identical struct from another instance of the type, either by copying a base without setting any field or by moving all fields individually.
Checks for unnecessary calls to ToOwned::to_owned and other to_owned-like functions.
Suggests removing an unnecessary trailing comma before the closing parenthesis in single-line macro invocations.
rules.bad: println!("Foo={}", 1,);

rules.description: Checks for calls of unwrap[_err]() that cannot fail.
Checks for private functions that only return Ok or Some.
Checks for structure field patterns bound to wildcards.
Checks for struct patterns that match against unit variant.

Checks for tuple patterns with a wildcard pattern (_) is next to a rest pattern (..). NOTE: While _, .. means there is at least one element left, .. means there are 0 or more elements left. This can make a difference when refactoring, but s

Checks for unnested or-patterns, e.g., Some(0) | Some(2) and suggests replacing the pattern with a nested one, Some(0 | 2). Another way to think of this is that it rewrites patterns in disjunctive normal form (DNF) into conjunctive normal f
Checks for usage of unreachable!.
rules.bad: unreachable!();

rules.description: Warns if a long integral or floating-point constant does not contain underscores.
Checks for deriving serde::Deserialize on a type that has methods using unsafe.
Checks for imports that remove “unsafe” from an item’s name.
Warns if literal suffixes are not separated by an underscore. To enforce unseparated literal suffix style, see the separated_literal_suffix lint.
Checks for transmutes between collections whose types have different ABI, size or alignment.
Checks for functions that are declared async but have no .awaits inside of them.
Checks for uses of the enumerate method where the index is unused (_)
Detects formatting parameters that have no effect on the output of format!(), println!() or similar macros.
rules.bad:
println!("{:.}", 1.0);

println!("not padded: {:5}", format_args!("..."));

rules.description: Checks for unused written/read amount.
Checks for the creation of a peekable iterator that is never .peek()ed
Checks for calls to Result::ok() without using the returned Option.
rules.bad: some_function().ok();

rules.description: Detects cases where a whole-number literal float is being rounded, using the floor, ceil, or round methods.
rules.bad: let x = 1f32.ceil();

rules.description: Checks methods that contain a self argument but don’t use it
Checks for use Trait where the Trait is only used for its methods and not referenced by a path directly.
Checks for unit (()) expressions that can be removed.
Warns if hexadecimal or binary literals are not grouped by nibble or byte.
rules.bad:
let x: u32 = 0xFFF_FFF;
let y: u8 = 0b01_011_101;

rules.description: Checks for functions of type Result that contain expect() or unwrap()
Checks for usages of the following functions with an argument that constructs a default value (e.g., Default::default or String::new): unwrap_or unwrap_or_else or_insert or_insert_with
rules.bad:
x.unwrap_or(Default::default());
map.entry(42).or_insert_with(String::new);

rules.description: Checks for .unwrap() or .unwrap_err() calls on Results and .unwrap() call on Options.
rules.bad:
option.unwrap();
result.unwrap();
rules.good:
option.expect("more helpful message");
result.expect("more helpful message");

rules.description: Checks for fully capitalized names and optionally names containing a capitalized acronym.
rules.bad: struct HTTPResponse;

rules.description: Checks for usage of Debug formatting. The purpose of this lint is to catch debugging remnants.
rules.bad: println!("{:?}", foo);

rules.description: Checks for unnecessary repetition of structure name when a replacement with Self is applicable.
Checks for the use of bindings with a single leading underscore.
Checks for the use of item with a single leading underscore.
Checks for usage of .as_ref() or .as_mut() where the types before and after the call are the same.

Checks for extern crate and use items annotated with lint attributes. This lint permits lint attributes for lints emitted on the items themself. For use items these lints are: ambiguous_glob_reexports dead_code deprecated hidden_glob_reexpo
Checks that the concat! macro has at least two arguments.
rules.bad: let x = concat!("a");

rules.description: Checks for Into, TryInto, From, TryFrom, or IntoIter calls which uselessly convert to the same type.
rules.bad:
// format!() returns a `String`
let s: String = format!("hello").into();

rules.description: Checks for the use of format!("string literal with no argument") and format!("{}", foo) where foo is a string.
rules.bad:
let foo = "foo";
format!("{}", foo);

rules.description: Checks for variable declarations immediately followed by a conditional affectation.
Checks for NonZero*::new_unchecked() being used in a const context.
rules.bad:
use std::num::NonZeroUsize;
const PLAYERS: NonZeroUsize = unsafe { NonZeroUsize::new_unchecked(3) };
rules.good:
use std::num::NonZeroUsize;
const PLAYERS: NonZeroUsize = NonZeroUsize::new(3).unwrap();

rules.description: Checks for transmutes to the original type of the object and transmutes that could be a cast.
rules.bad: core::intrinsics::transmute(t); // where the result type is the same as `t`'s

rules.description: Checks for usage of vec![..] when using [..] would be possible.
Checks for usage of Vec<Box<T>> where T: Sized anywhere in the code. Check the Box documentation for more information.

Checks for calls to push immediately after creating a new Vec. If the Vec is created using with_capacity this will only lint if the capacity is a constant and the number of pushes is greater than or equal to the initial capacity. If the Vec
Finds occurrences of Vec::resize(0, an_int)
Checks for bit masks that can be replaced by a call to trailing_zeros
Checks for usage of File::read_to_end and File::read_to_string.
This lint warns when volatile load/store operations (write_volatile/read_volatile) are applied to composite types.
Checks for usage of waker.clone().wake()
rules.bad: waker.clone().wake();

rules.description: Checks for while loops comparing floating point values.
Checks whether variables used within while loop condition can be (and are) mutated in the body.
Detects loop + match combinations that are easier written as a while let loop.
Checks for while let expressions on iterators.
Checks for wildcard dependencies in the Cargo.toml.
rules.bad:
[dependencies]
regex = "*"

rules.description: Checks for wildcard enum matches using _.
Checks for wildcard imports use _::*.
rules.bad:
use crate1::foo; // Imports a function named foo
use crate2::*; // Has a function named foo

foo(); // Calls crate1::foo

rules.description: Checks for wildcard pattern used with others patterns in same match arm.
This lint warns about the use of literals as write!/writeln! args.
rules.bad: writeln!(buf, "{}", "foo");

rules.description: This lint warns when you use write!() with a format string that ends in a newline.
rules.bad: write!(buf, "Hello {}!\n", name);

rules.description: This lint warns when you use writeln!(buf, "") to print a newline.
rules.bad: writeln!(buf, "");

rules.description: Checks for methods with certain name prefixes or suffixes, and which do not adhere to standard conventions regarding how self is taken. The actual rules are: PrefixPostfixself takenself type as_none&self or &mut selfany from_nonenoneany int
Checks for transmutes that can’t ever be correct on any architecture.
rules.bad: let ptr: *const T = core::intrinsics::transmute('x')

rules.description: Checks for 0.0 / 0.0.
Warns if an integral constant literal starts with 0.
Catch casts from 0 to some pointer type
rules.bad: let a = 0 as *const u32;

rules.description: Checks for array or vec initializations which contain an expression with side effects, but which have a repeat count of zero.
Checks for maps with zero-sized value types anywhere in the code.
Looks for code that spawns a process but never calls wait() on the child.
rules.bad:
use std::process::Command;

let _child = Command::new("ls").spawn().expect("failed to execute child");

rules.description: Checks for offset(_), wrapping_{add, sub}, etc. on raw pointers to zero-sized types
rules.bad: unsafe { (&() as *const ()).offset(1) };
